original_hypothesis.title: changeBridgedToken Deletes Wrong Mapping Key Leaving Stale Canonical Reference
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken, the code executes `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` which is a no-op since _btokenNew was verified to have no existing mapping (bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew].addr == address(0)). The intended deletion should be `delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]` to clean up the old bridged token's canonical mapping. After migration, btokenOld_ retains its bridgedToCanonical mapping despite being blacklisted.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect. The stale mapping means bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] still maps to the canonical token, breaking the bijection invariant between bridgedToCanonical and canonicalToBridged. While the blacklist prevents sendToken usage, the stale data persists in storage and could cause issues in future upgrades or off-chain systems.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] is no-op; should be delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]

preconditions: Owner calls changeBridgedToken with existing old bridged token

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] persists with stale canonical token data after migration
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No post-condition check that old mapping was cleared

analysis_questions: Can the stale mapping be exploited to bypass blacklist in any code path?
Does _handleMessage check blacklist before or after checking bridgedToCanonical?
Could a future upgrade reference stale bridgedToCanonical data?
validation_requirements: Check all code paths that read bridgedToCanonical for blacklist bypass potential
Confirm storage inconsistency between bridgedToCanonical and canonicalToBridged

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner migrates token. Old token retains canonical mapping. If blacklist check is ever bypassed or removed in upgrade, old token could be used to burn and trigger transfers based on stale mapping.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Storage inconsistency; potential double-accounting if blacklist is bypassed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the migrated token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to clean stale mapping

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] cleanup after migration

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Bug triggered on every changeBridgedToken call
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Verifiable via storage read after changeBridgedToken

validation_result.impact_summary: changeBridgedToken deletes bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] (a no-op) instead of bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_], leaving stale canonical mapping data for the old bridged token after migration.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The function changeBridgedToken in ERC20Vault.sol contains a clear code defect. At the top of the function, the code verifies that `bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew].addr != address(0)` would cause a revert — meaning _btokenNew is confirmed to have NO existing canonical mapping. Later in the function body, `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` is executed, which is a no-op since the mapping was already empty/zero. The correct line should be `delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]` to clean up the old bridged token's canonical mapping. After execution, bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] retains stale canonical token data even though canonicalToBridged no longer points back to btokenOld_. This breaks the bijection invariant between bridgedToCanonical and canonicalToBridged. While the btokenBlacklist prevents direct exploitation via sendToken, the stale mapping persists in storage.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is unambiguous. The precondition check at the function entry (`bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew].addr != address(0)` reverts) proves that `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` is always a no-op when reached. The intended target `btokenOld_` is never cleaned from bridgedToCanonical.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The precondition check guarantees bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew].addr == address(0). Therefore `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` is a no-op. The intended deletion target is bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_], which retains stale data after migration. This breaks the bidirectional mapping invariant.

calling_sequence: 2. Function validates _btokenNew has no existing bridgedToCanonical mapping (addr == address(0))
4. If btokenOld_ != address(0), enters the if-block
5. Reads ctoken from bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] for validation
6. Executes `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` — no-op since _btokenNew mapping is empty
8. Sets migration statuses on old and new tokens
11. Result: bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] still contains stale canonical token data
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerable path is always taken when changeBridgedToken is called with an existing old bridged token (btokenOld_ != address(0)). The delete statement on line ~188 operates on _btokenNew instead of btokenOld_. Since _btokenNew was validated to have an empty mapping entry at function entry (line ~162: `bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew].addr != address(0)` causes revert), the delete is a no-op. bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] is never cleared.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: DeployUSDCAdapter.s.sol calls ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken(canonicalToken, adapterProxy), confirming the function is used in production deployment. This would trigger the bug, leaving stale bridgedToCanonical entries for any previously deployed bridged token.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: After changeBridgedToken is called: (1) bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] still maps to the canonical token. (2) btokenOld_ is blacklisted, preventing sendToken usage. (3) However, _handleMessage reads bridgedToCanonical to determine if a token is bridged: `if (bridgedToCanonical[_token].addr != address(0))`. If the blacklist check were ever bypassed or removed in an upgrade, the stale mapping could allow btokenOld_ to be treated as a valid bridged token. (4) The stale data could also cause issues in off-chain indexers or future upgrade logic that iterates over or queries bridgedToCanonical without checking the blacklist.
validation_result.impact_assessment: This is a confirmed code defect (wrong variable in delete statement). The immediate practical impact is limited because the blacklist prevents direct use of btokenOld_ via sendToken. However: (1) Storage inconsistency persists permanently unless manually corrected via upgrade. (2) The bridgedToCanonical ↔ canonicalToBridged bijection invariant is broken. (3) Any future code path or upgrade that reads bridgedToCanonical without checking btokenBlacklist could be affected. (4) The stale entry wastes storage and could mislead off-chain systems. This is a real bug regardless of current exploitability.
recommended_mitigations: Add a post-condition assertion: `assert(bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_].addr == address(0))` after the delete
For already-deployed contracts, issue an upgrade that cleans stale bridgedToCanonical entries for blacklisted tokens
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ERC20Vault and a BridgedERC20 (btokenOld). 2. Set up bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld] and canonicalToBridged mappings. 3. Deploy a new BridgedERC20 (btokenNew). 4. Call changeBridgedToken with the canonical token and btokenNew. 5. Assert bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld].addr != address(0) — this will pass, proving the stale mapping persists. 6. Assert bridgedToCanonical[btokenNew].addr == _ctoken.addr — this will pass, confirming the new mapping is set correctly. 7. The test confirms the bijection is broken.
uncertainties: Whether any current code path can bypass the btokenBlacklist check to exploit the stale mapping
Whether off-chain systems currently rely on bridgedToCanonical for indexing in ways that would be affected
additional_testing_needed: Verify all code paths that read bridgedToCanonical to ensure none bypass the blacklist
Test changeBridgedToken and verify bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] cleanup
Fuzz test migration scenarios to check for accounting inconsistencies caused by stale mappings
